1: Ps 149: New Song, Two-Edged Sword — Praise and Eschatological Judgment. Ps 149 is the most tension-filled of the five final Hallelujah psalms. It pairs the worship assembly's joy (vv1-5) with their role as YHWH's agents of judgment (vv6-9), holding praise and divine warrior imagery together. The 'new song' (shir chadash) appears in Psalms 33:3; Psalms 40:3; Psalms 96:1; Psalms 98:1; 144:9 and Revelation 5:9; Psalms 14:3 — it is the song of unprecedented divine deliverance. 'Praise the LORD! Sing to the LORD a new song, his praise in the assembly of the godly!' — the 'assembly of the godly' (qehal chasidim) — the covenant-loyal ones (chasidim, from chesed) as the specific worshiping community. Their assembly is the setting for the new song.
2: 'Let Israel be glad in his Maker; let the children of Zion rejoice in their King!' — 'glad in his Maker' (yismach Yisrael beosav) — the creator-creature joy: Israel's gladness is in the one who made them. 'Rejoice in their King' (yigilu venei Tzion beMalkam) — the children of Zion (the covenant community in Jerusalem) rejoice in YHWH as their king. The Davidic-YHWH kingship theology: YHWH is Israel's true king whose human representative is the Davidic king (1 Samuel 8:7: 'they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them').
3: 'Let them praise his name with dancing, making melody to him with tambourine and lyre!' — dance (machol) and the tambourine (toph) — the most festive, embodied form of worship. The dancing praise of Miriam at the sea (Exodus 15:20) and David before the ark (2 Samuel 6:14) are the biblical models. Praise that is fully embodied, not merely verbal or mental. The lyre (kinnor) provides the harmonic complement to the percussive tambourine.
4: 'For the LORD takes pleasure in his people; he adorns the humble with salvation' — 'takes pleasure in his people' (ki rotze YHWH beamo) — the divine delight in the covenant community (cf. Psalms 147:11: YHWH's pleasure in those who fear him). 'Adorns the humble with salvation' (yephaer anavim beyeshu'ah) — the anavim (humble/meek) receive salvation (yeshu'ah) as adornment (pe'er, beauty/glory): their salvation is not merely rescue but transformation into beauty. The anavim were the Beatitude-people ('blessed are the meek') before the NT; Ps 149 is their promised adornment.
5: 'Let the godly exult in glory; let them sing for joy on their beds' — 'sing for joy on their beds' (yirannenu al-mishkevotam) — the intimate dimension of praise: even in the private resting place, even at night, the chasidim sing. The contrast with the public assembly (v1) and the battle (vv6-9): praise penetrates every setting, private and public, peaceful and combative.
6: 'Let the high praises of God be in their throats and two-edged swords in their hands' — the most striking verse of the psalm: the union of worship and warfare. 'High praises of God' (romemot El bigeronam) — exaltations of God in their throats: the same organ that produces praise also breathes battle. 'Two-edged swords' (cherev piphiyot) in their hands simultaneously. The combination has both historical and eschatological dimensions: Nehemiah's wall-builders held a tool in one hand and a weapon in the other (Nehemiah 4:17); the eschatological army praises as it fights.
7: '...to execute vengeance on the nations and punishments on the peoples' — 'execute vengeance on the nations' (la'asot neqamah bagoyim tochechot bale'umim) — the covenant warrior language: YHWH's vengeance (neqamah) executed through the covenant people's action. The OT understanding of holy war: Israel as the instrument of divine judgment on nations that have accumulated covenant-violating sins (Genesis 15:16: 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete'). Paul redefines this warfare as spiritual in Ephesians 6:10-18 and 2 Corinthians 10:4.
8: '...to bind their kings with chains and their nobles with fetters of iron' — 'bind their kings with chains' (le'esor malkeihem biziqim venichbadeihem bekhavle barzel) — the political dimension of the divine warrior victory: the kings and nobles who have defied YHWH's sovereignty will be bound. The iron chains echo both the Egyptian bondage of Israel (which YHWH broke) and the Assyrian-Babylonian captivity of Israel's kings (which YHWH allowed). The reversal: those who held others captive are themselves bound.
9: '...to execute on them the judgment written! This is honor for all his godly ones. Praise the LORD!' — 'the judgment written' (hamishpat katuv) — the pre-written judgment: the execution of what YHWH has decreed in his heavenly law. The book-of-judgment imagery (Daniel 7:10; Revelation 20:12) — YHWH's decrees are not improvised but enacted from prior written record. 'Honor for all his godly ones' (hadar hu lechol chasidav) — the final paradox: participating in the divine judgment is itself the dignity of the chasidim. The closing 'Hallelujah!' holds the paradox: praise and judgment are not opposites in the Psalter's theology.
